title: Barringtonia arborea
intro: Barringtonia arborea, also known as the Barringtonia tree or fish poison tree, is a medium-sized evergreen tree native to tropical regions of Asia and the Pacific Islands. The tree has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antifungal properties, which help to alleviate conditions such as fever, rheumatism, and skin infections. The bioactive constituents of Barringtonia arborea include alkaloids, flavonoids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the tree is commonly processed into herbal preparations such as decoctions, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat a range of health issues.
taxonomy: Barringtonia arborea is classified within the plant kingdom as a species of tree. It belongs to the family Lecythidaceae and is also known as the sea poison tree or candle nut tree. This tree is native to tropical Asia and is commonly found in India, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific Islands. It is a large evergreen tree that can grow up to 15 meters in height. The classification of Barringtonia arborea at the species level is Barringtonia arborea (L.) Roxb.

side_effects: Barringtonia arborea, commonly known as the sea poison tree or strychnos tree, has been reported to have various side effects due to its toxicity. The seeds of the plant contain a toxic compound called strychnine, which can be fatal if ingested. Ingesting any part of the plant can cause severe symptoms, including nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. Long-term exposure to the plant's toxic compounds may also lead to neurological damage and seizures. The plant's toxicity requires caution when handling or approaching it.
